name: Eddie South
abstract: Edward Otha South (November 27, 1904 – April 25, 1962) was an American jazz violinist.

works.t: Violin Concerto No. 2 in B Minor, Op. 7: 3. La campanella
Babes in Arms: Where or When

about_quotes.speaker: Milt Hinton
about_quotes.evidence: in the 30s I remember I was working with Eddie South, a great violin player, and I got the job to play with Eddie
about_quotes.doc_title: Milt Hinton interviewed by Joe Williams, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: the next real job I had was with Eddie South... he only took a trio on the road and the trio was piano, bass, and violin... I did some trio recordings with Eddie South
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Hinton Milt
about_quotes.evidence: Everett Boxdale, Eddie South, and myself. We got a job at the… at least I got a job in a club on Sunset Boulevard called the Club Ballyhoo
about_quotes.doc_title: Hinton Milt — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Al Hall
about_quotes.evidence: I played with all the noted violinists through Eddie South and Joe Venuti and Ray Nance and --

about_quotes.speaker: Danny Barker
about_quotes.evidence: I did reach the age where I did play with him. Because when Eddie South came back from Europe... he was supposed to organize a big band... And he got really the best musicians available around Chicago at the time... Well, we rehearsed... Eddie South is still in Europe, and this band is going to rehe

about_quotes.speaker: Ernie Fields
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript mentions Eddie South in the context of Rozelle Claxton's decision to leave Ernie Fields' band for South's band. Ernie Fields (the interviewee) does not describe a direct personal or professional relationship with Eddie South, only referencing him as the leader of the band Claxton join
